notes: These files are designed for rendering detailed type information, not just range checking.
Fields are intentionally verbose so the UI can choose what to show.
mainstreamSizes describes common ABIs/platforms; standardGuarantee describes what the language standard itself promises.
languageNotes: This file intentionally mixes C and C++ because the current page exposes them as one language choice.
Every entry states whether it is standard C, standard C++, both, or an extension / POSIX type.
For C and C++ integer widths, the standard mostly specifies ordering and minimum widths rather than fixed byte counts.
For exact-width typedefs like int32_t, availability depends on the implementation actually having a type with exactly that width and no padding bits.

types.availability: C: _Bool is built-in since C99; bool is macro alias from <stdbool.h> through C17 and a keyword in C23. C++: bool is a built-in fundamental type.
types.definition:
Boolean type. In C, the built-in type is `_Bool`; the identifier `bool` historically came from `<stdbool.h>` and in C23 becomes a language keyword.
In C++, `bool` is a distinct fundamental type with the values `true` and `false`.
types.summary:
Use for logical state, conditions, flags, and APIs that conceptually answer yes/no. This is not a 1-bit storage type;
it is a proper object type that stores one of two semantic values. In practice it usually occupies one byte, but the standard does not require a 1-byte object representation.
types.standardGuarantee:
Standard guarantee: values are `0`/`1` in C for `_Bool` after conversion, and `false`/`true` in C++. Any scalar converted to boolean becomes false if it compares equal to zero and true otherwise.
Size is implementation-defined. The standard does not promise a fixed width in bits beyond representing the two values.
specialRules: Converting any nonzero integer or floating value to bool yields true; zero yields false.
In C, `_Bool x = 2;` stores 1, not 2.
In C++, `bool` participates in integral promotions; arithmetic on bool is legal but usually stylistically discouraged.
Never use bool when bit-level layout must be exact across ABIs unless you have a separate serialization contract.
commonConfusions: `bool` is not guaranteed to be 8 bits by the language standard even if it usually is.
`bool` is not an integer width substitute like `uint8_t`.

types.definition:
`char` is a distinct integer type used for character code units and raw bytes. It is not the same type as `signed char` or `unsigned char`,
even though it has the same size and alignment as one of them.
types.summary:
This is one of the most important edge-case types in C and C++. Plain `char` is its own type, but whether it behaves as signed or unsigned for numeric conversion is implementation-defined.
It is also the canonical type for narrow character literals and for many byte-oriented APIs.
types.standardGuarantee:
Standard guarantee: `sizeof(char) == 1` by definition. A byte in C/C++ is the size of `char`; it is not guaranteed to be 8 bits by the language standard,
though `CHAR_BIT` is at least 8 and on mainstream systems is 8. Signedness of plain `char` is implementation-defined.
types.representation:
Same object representation and alignment as either `signed char` or `unsigned char`. It can alias object representations byte-wise.
In ordinary hosted desktop/server environments it is almost always 8 bits.

mainstreamSizes.size: 1 byte, but a byte may be >8 bits

specialRules: Plain `char` may be signed or unsigned depending on the compiler/target.
Do not use plain `char` for arithmetic that depends on negative values unless you know the implementation choice.
`char`, `signed char`, and `unsigned char` are three distinct types.
Access through character types is special in aliasing rules; byte-wise inspection of object representations is typically done through character types.
commonConfusions: `char` is not guaranteed to be signed.
`sizeof(char)` is always 1, but that does not imply 8 bits.
Text encoding is separate from the type width.
readMore: basic character and object representation rules

types.definition: Signed integer type with the same size as `char`. Distinct from both `char` and `unsigned char`.
types.summary: Use this when you want a byte-sized signed integer and you really mean signed arithmetic. It is preferable to plain `char` when negative values matter semantically.
types.standardGuarantee:
Standard guarantee: width is the same as `char`; minimum range is at least -127..127 in pre-C23 wording and effectively two's-complement range on mainstream systems.
Since modern C and C++ practice assumes two's-complement, mainstream behavior is -128..127 for 8-bit bytes.
specialRules: Overflow of signed integer arithmetic is undefined behavior in C and C++ for the built-in arithmetic types.
Because it is a signed integer type, promotion to `int` often happens before arithmetic.
Use this rather than plain `char` when you need explicit signed semantics.

types.definition: Unsigned integer type with the same size as `char`. Distinct from both `char` and `signed char`.
types.summary:
This is the canonical safe choice when you need a byte-sized integer with modular arithmetic and values 0..UCHAR_MAX.
It is also commonly used for raw binary data, byte buffers, and object representation inspection.
types.standardGuarantee:
Standard guarantee: same size as `char`; minimum maximum value is at least 255 if `CHAR_BIT >= 8`. On mainstream systems it is 8-bit and ranges 0..255.
Unsigned arithmetic is modulo 2^N where N is the width of the type.
specialRules: Unsigned overflow is defined wraparound modulo 2^N.
`unsigned char` is often the least surprising byte type for binary data.
Do not confuse storage of bytes with text encoding semantics.

types.availability: C: typedef in <stddef.h> / <wchar.h>. C++: fundamental distinct type.
types.kind: wide character / implementation-defined integer-like character type
types.definition:
Type intended to hold wide character codes / wide code units. In C it is a typedef name provided by the library; in C++ it is a distinct fundamental type.
Its width, signedness, and encoding relationship are implementation-defined.
types.summary:
Historically used for wide-character APIs, locale-sensitive text, and platform-native wide text interfaces.
It is extremely platform-specific: 16-bit on Windows, usually 32-bit on Unix-like systems. Therefore it should not be treated as a portable Unicode code point type without checking the platform contract.
types.standardGuarantee:
Standard guarantee: enough to represent all members of the implementation's largest extended character set among the supported locales, subject to the implementation's model.
The exact width and signedness are implementation-defined. No fixed Unicode width is promised by the language.
specialRules: Portable code should not assume that `wchar_t` is Unicode scalar value sized.
On Windows, one `wchar_t` may hold only one UTF-16 code unit, not necessarily a full Unicode code point.
In C++, `wchar_t` is a distinct fundamental type and not just a typedef.

types.availability: C: keyword/type in C23. C++: distinct fundamental type since C++20.
types.definition:
Type intended for UTF-8 code units. It is not the same as `char` or `unsigned char`.
In modern C++ it supports `u8` string literal elements; in C23 a corresponding type also exists.
types.summary: Use when you want code to say explicitly 'this object stores UTF-8 code units'. It improves semantic clarity over plain `char` and helps separate text encoding from raw bytes.
types.standardGuarantee: Standard guarantee: width is 8 bits where the type exists for UTF-8 code units. The intent is code-unit semantics, not arbitrary locale-dependent narrow execution encoding semantics.

mainstreamSizes.platform: modern compilers with C23 / C++20 support

specialRules: Distinct from `char`; overload resolution and template matching in C++ will distinguish them.
A UTF-8 code point may require multiple `char8_t` code units.
Existing APIs that take `char*` will usually need explicit adaptation.

types.availability: C: type since C11. C++: distinct fundamental type since C++11.
types.definition: Type for UTF-16 code units. Distinct from `unsigned short` and from other character types.
types.summary: This is a code-unit type, not a promise that one object stores one Unicode code point. Supplementary characters require surrogate pairs.
types.standardGuarantee: Standard guarantee: large enough and intended for UTF-16 code units. On mainstream implementations it is 16 bits.
specialRules: One Unicode scalar value may require two `char16_t` values.
Distinct type semantics matter for overloads and templates.

types.availability: C: type since C11. C++: distinct fundamental type since C++11.
types.definition: Type for UTF-32 code units. Distinct from `unsigned int` and from other character types.
types.summary: Usually the least ambiguous standard character type for storing a full Unicode scalar value directly in one object, though normalization and other text semantics still remain separate concerns.
types.standardGuarantee: Standard guarantee: large enough and intended for UTF-32 code units. On mainstream implementations it is 32 bits.
specialRules: Code-unit semantics are still distinct from higher-level text semantics such as grapheme clusters.
Distinct type semantics matter for overloads and templates.

types.definition: short integer type. Spelling variants like `signed short` and `short int` refer to the same type.
types.summary:
Core arithmetic integer type in the standard rank hierarchy. This is one of the built-in integer types used for ordinary numeric work and integer promotions.
On mainstream systems the width is commonly 16 bits or more depending on the rank.
types.standardGuarantee:
Standard guarantee: minimum width is 16 bits. The standard specifies relative ordering of widths/ranks rather than a single universal size for all targets.
Mainstream practice is [{'platform': 'LP64 / LLP64 / ILP32 mainstream', 'size': '2 bytes / 16 bits'}]. Signed overflow is undefined behavior for built-in signed arithmetic; unsigned arithmetic is modulo 2^N.
specialRules: Integer promotions often convert narrower integer types to `int` or `unsigned int` before arithmetic.
The exact ABI size is implementation-defined except for fixed-width typedef families like int32_t when they exist.
Use exact-width typedefs for serialization/protocol/layout requirements; use built-in rank types for natural machine arithmetic.
commonConfusions: `short` is not guaranteed to have exactly 16 bits; 16 is a floor, not a universal exact size.

types.definition: short integer type. Spelling variants like `signed short` and `short int` refer to the same type.
types.summary:
Core arithmetic integer type in the standard rank hierarchy. This is one of the built-in integer types used for ordinary numeric work and integer promotions.
On mainstream systems the width is commonly 16 bits or more depending on the rank.
types.standardGuarantee:
Standard guarantee: minimum width is 16 bits. The standard specifies relative ordering of widths/ranks rather than a single universal size for all targets.
Mainstream practice is [{'platform': 'LP64 / LLP64 / ILP32 mainstream', 'size': '2 bytes / 16 bits'}]. Signed overflow is undefined behavior for built-in signed arithmetic; unsigned arithmetic is modulo 2^N.
specialRules: Integer promotions often convert narrower integer types to `int` or `unsigned int` before arithmetic.
The exact ABI size is implementation-defined except for fixed-width typedef families like int32_t when they exist.
Use exact-width typedefs for serialization/protocol/layout requirements; use built-in rank types for natural machine arithmetic.
Unsigned overflow wraps modulo 2^N.
commonConfusions: `unsigned short` is not guaranteed to have exactly 16 bits; 16 is a floor, not a universal exact size.

types.definition: int integer type. Spelling variants like `signed int` and `int int` refer to the same type.
types.summary:
Core arithmetic integer type in the standard rank hierarchy. This is one of the built-in integer types used for ordinary numeric work and integer promotions.
On mainstream systems the width is commonly 16 bits or more depending on the rank.
types.standardGuarantee:
Standard guarantee: minimum width is 16 bits. The standard specifies relative ordering of widths/ranks rather than a single universal size for all targets.
Mainstream practice is [{'platform': 'LP64 Unix-like', 'size': '4 bytes / 32 bits'}, {'platform': 'LLP64 Windows', 'size': '4 bytes / 32 bits'}, {'platform': 'ILP32 32-bit targets', 'size': '4 bytes / 32 bits'}, {'platform': 'small embedded targets', 'size': 'may be 2 bytes / 16 bits'}]. Signed overflow is undefined behavior for built-in signed arithmetic; unsigned arithmetic is modulo 2^N.
specialRules: Integer promotions often convert narrower integer types to `int` or `unsigned int` before arithmetic.
The exact ABI size is implementation-defined except for fixed-width typedef families like int32_t when they exist.
Use exact-width typedefs for serialization/protocol/layout requirements; use built-in rank types for natural machine arithmetic.
commonConfusions: `int` is not guaranteed to have exactly 16 bits; 16 is a floor, not a universal exact size.

types.definition: int integer type. Spelling variants like `signed int` and `int int` refer to the same type.
types.summary:
Core arithmetic integer type in the standard rank hierarchy. This is one of the built-in integer types used for ordinary numeric work and integer promotions.
On mainstream systems the width is commonly 16 bits or more depending on the rank.
types.standardGuarantee:
Standard guarantee: minimum width is 16 bits. The standard specifies relative ordering of widths/ranks rather than a single universal size for all targets.
Mainstream practice is [{'platform': 'LP64 Unix-like', 'size': '4 bytes / 32 bits'}, {'platform': 'LLP64 Windows', 'size': '4 bytes / 32 bits'}, {'platform': 'small embedded targets', 'size': 'may be 2 bytes / 16 bits'}]. Signed overflow is undefined behavior for built-in signed arithmetic; unsigned arithmetic is modulo 2^N.
specialRules: Integer promotions often convert narrower integer types to `int` or `unsigned int` before arithmetic.
The exact ABI size is implementation-defined except for fixed-width typedef families like int32_t when they exist.
Use exact-width typedefs for serialization/protocol/layout requirements; use built-in rank types for natural machine arithmetic.
Unsigned overflow wraps modulo 2^N; this is defined behavior.
commonConfusions: `unsigned int` is not guaranteed to have exactly 16 bits; 16 is a floor, not a universal exact size.

types.definition: long integer type. Spelling variants like `signed long` and `long int` refer to the same type.
types.summary:
Core arithmetic integer type in the standard rank hierarchy. This is one of the built-in integer types used for ordinary numeric work and integer promotions.
On mainstream systems the width is commonly 32 bits or more depending on the rank.
types.standardGuarantee:
Standard guarantee: minimum width is 32 bits. The standard specifies relative ordering of widths/ranks rather than a single universal size for all targets.
Mainstream practice is [{'platform': 'LP64 Unix-like', 'size': '8 bytes / 64 bits'}, {'platform': 'LLP64 Windows', 'size': '4 bytes / 32 bits'}, {'platform': 'ILP32 32-bit targets', 'size': '4 bytes / 32 bits'}]. Signed overflow is undefined behavior for built-in signed arithmetic; unsigned arithmetic is modulo 2^N.
specialRules: Integer promotions often convert narrower integer types to `int` or `unsigned int` before arithmetic.
The exact ABI size is implementation-defined except for fixed-width typedef families like int32_t when they exist.
Use exact-width typedefs for serialization/protocol/layout requirements; use built-in rank types for natural machine arithmetic.
commonConfusions: `long` is not guaranteed to have exactly 32 bits; 32 is a floor, not a universal exact size.

types.definition: long integer type. Spelling variants like `signed long` and `long int` refer to the same type.
types.summary:
Core arithmetic integer type in the standard rank hierarchy. This is one of the built-in integer types used for ordinary numeric work and integer promotions.
On mainstream systems the width is commonly 32 bits or more depending on the rank.
types.standardGuarantee:
Standard guarantee: minimum width is 32 bits. The standard specifies relative ordering of widths/ranks rather than a single universal size for all targets.
Mainstream practice is [{'platform': 'LP64 Unix-like', 'size': '8 bytes / 64 bits'}, {'platform': 'LLP64 Windows', 'size': '4 bytes / 32 bits'}, {'platform': 'ILP32 32-bit targets', 'size': '4 bytes / 32 bits'}]. Signed overflow is undefined behavior for built-in signed arithmetic; unsigned arithmetic is modulo 2^N.
specialRules: Integer promotions often convert narrower integer types to `int` or `unsigned int` before arithmetic.
The exact ABI size is implementation-defined except for fixed-width typedef families like int32_t when they exist.
Use exact-width typedefs for serialization/protocol/layout requirements; use built-in rank types for natural machine arithmetic.
Unsigned overflow wraps modulo 2^N.
commonConfusions: `unsigned long` is not guaranteed to have exactly 32 bits; 32 is a floor, not a universal exact size.

types.definition: long long integer type. Spelling variants like `signed long long` and `long long int` refer to the same type.
types.summary:
Core arithmetic integer type in the standard rank hierarchy. This is one of the built-in integer types used for ordinary numeric work and integer promotions.
On mainstream systems the width is commonly 64 bits or more depending on the rank.
types.standardGuarantee:
Standard guarantee: minimum width is 64 bits. The standard specifies relative ordering of widths/ranks rather than a single universal size for all targets.
Mainstream practice is [{'platform': 'LP64 / LLP64 / ILP32 mainstream', 'size': '8 bytes / 64 bits'}]. Signed overflow is undefined behavior for built-in signed arithmetic; unsigned arithmetic is modulo 2^N.
specialRules: Integer promotions often convert narrower integer types to `int` or `unsigned int` before arithmetic.
The exact ABI size is implementation-defined except for fixed-width typedef families like int32_t when they exist.
Use exact-width typedefs for serialization/protocol/layout requirements; use built-in rank types for natural machine arithmetic.
commonConfusions: `long long` is not guaranteed to have exactly 64 bits; 64 is a floor, not a universal exact size.

types.definition: long long integer type. Spelling variants like `signed long long` and `long long int` refer to the same type.
types.summary:
Core arithmetic integer type in the standard rank hierarchy. This is one of the built-in integer types used for ordinary numeric work and integer promotions.
On mainstream systems the width is commonly 64 bits or more depending on the rank.
types.standardGuarantee:
Standard guarantee: minimum width is 64 bits. The standard specifies relative ordering of widths/ranks rather than a single universal size for all targets.
Mainstream practice is [{'platform': 'LP64 / LLP64 / ILP32 mainstream', 'size': '8 bytes / 64 bits'}]. Signed overflow is undefined behavior for built-in signed arithmetic; unsigned arithmetic is modulo 2^N.
specialRules: Integer promotions often convert narrower integer types to `int` or `unsigned int` before arithmetic.
The exact ABI size is implementation-defined except for fixed-width typedef families like int32_t when they exist.
Use exact-width typedefs for serialization/protocol/layout requirements; use built-in rank types for natural machine arithmetic.
Unsigned overflow wraps modulo 2^N.
commonConfusions: `unsigned long long` is not guaranteed to have exactly 64 bits; 64 is a floor, not a universal exact size.

types.availability: C: typedef in <stddef.h> and related headers. C++: typedef / using in <cstddef> and other library headers, often visible as `std::size_t`.
types.definition:
Unsigned integer type returned by `sizeof` and used to represent sizes of objects in bytes.
In C++ user code most explicitly sees `std::size_t`, though plain `size_t` may be injected by headers.
types.summary:
Use for array sizes, buffer lengths, object sizes, and container size APIs. It is the natural type for byte counts from the language/library point of view,
but it is not automatically the right choice for every integer loop or difference calculation.
types.standardGuarantee:
Standard guarantee: large enough to represent the size in bytes of any object that can exist in the implementation.
Exact width is implementation-defined and tracks the data model / ABI. It is usually the same width as a pointer-sized unsigned integer on mainstream systems.
specialRules: It is unsigned. Subtracting two sizes can underflow if the result is negative.
Mixing signed loop indices with `size_t` often produces signed/unsigned comparison warnings or bugs.
Prefer `ptrdiff_t` for pointer differences or signed sizes that may become negative.
`size_t` is an alias / typedef, not a separate fundamental arithmetic category.
commonConfusions: `size_t` is not guaranteed to be the same as `unsigned long` or `unsigned long long` on all platforms.
`size_t` is about sizes, not necessarily about every pointer round-trip; for that see `uintptr_t` when available.

types.availability: C: typedef in <stddef.h>. C++: typedef / using in <cstddef>.
types.definition: Signed integer type of the result of subtracting two pointers to elements of the same array object (or one past the end) when the subtraction is well-defined.
types.summary: Use when you need a signed distance between positions in the same object range. It is the signed counterpart conceptually adjacent to `size_t`, but not simply 'signed size_t' by rule.
types.standardGuarantee:
Standard guarantee: able to represent the result of subtracting two pointers into the same array object, subject to the implementation's limits.
Exact width is implementation-defined, usually pointer-sized signed integer width on mainstream platforms.
specialRules: Pointer subtraction is only defined within the same array object (including one-past-end).
`ptrdiff_t` can be narrower than theoretical address space on unusual targets, but must be sufficient for valid same-array pointer differences under the implementation's model.
Do not use it for arbitrary integer serialization layout unless you control the ABI.

types.availability: POSIX / Unix extension; not standard C or standard C++
types.definition: POSIX signed counterpart commonly paired with APIs that return either a nonnegative byte count or -1 for error.
types.summary: Frequently seen in Unix system interfaces such as `read`, `write`, and related APIs. Very common in practice, but not part of ISO C or ISO C++.
types.standardGuarantee: No ISO C/C++ guarantee. On mainstream POSIX systems it is usually the signed type corresponding to the width of `size_t`.
specialRules: Treat as platform / POSIX API type, not portable ISO-language core type.
Useful when a nonnegative count and a negative sentinel must coexist in one return type.

types.availability: C: <stdint.h> if provided. C++: <cstdint> if provided.
types.definition: Exact-width signed integer typedef with exactly 8 value bits and no padding bits, if the implementation provides it.
types.summary: Preferred type when serialized layout, protocol fields, binary file formats, SIMD lane widths, or cross-language ABI contracts need an exact signed width of 8 bits.
types.standardGuarantee:
Standard guarantee: if `int8_t` exists, it has exactly 8 bits of width and two's-complement-style ordinary range behavior for that width on mainstream implementations.
The standard does not require every implementation to provide every exact-width typedef unless the implementation has a suitable underlying type.
specialRules: Existence is conditional on implementation support.
Best choice for wire formats and binary interchange when available.
Still participates in integer promotions.

types.availability: C: <stdint.h> if provided. C++: <cstdint> if provided.
types.definition: Exact-width unsigned integer typedef with exactly 8 bits, if the implementation provides it.
types.summary: Preferred unsigned exact-width type for packet headers, file formats, hashes, low-level bit manipulation, and stable binary layout of 8 bits.
types.standardGuarantee:
Standard guarantee: if `uint8_t` exists, it has exactly 8 bits. Unsigned arithmetic is modulo 2^8.
The standard only requires it when the implementation has a suitable underlying type with that exact width and no padding bits.
specialRules: Existence is conditional on implementation support.
Overflow is defined wrap modulo 2^8.
Excellent for bit-level protocols and binary interchange.

types.availability: C: <stdint.h> if provided. C++: <cstdint> if provided.
types.definition: Exact-width signed integer typedef with exactly 16 value bits and no padding bits, if the implementation provides it.
types.summary: Preferred type when serialized layout, protocol fields, binary file formats, SIMD lane widths, or cross-language ABI contracts need an exact signed width of 16 bits.
types.standardGuarantee:
Standard guarantee: if `int16_t` exists, it has exactly 16 bits of width and two's-complement-style ordinary range behavior for that width on mainstream implementations.
The standard does not require every implementation to provide every exact-width typedef unless the implementation has a suitable underlying type.
specialRules: Existence is conditional on implementation support.
Best choice for wire formats and binary interchange when available.
Still participates in integer promotions.

types.availability: C: <stdint.h> if provided. C++: <cstdint> if provided.
types.definition: Exact-width unsigned integer typedef with exactly 16 bits, if the implementation provides it.
types.summary: Preferred unsigned exact-width type for packet headers, file formats, hashes, low-level bit manipulation, and stable binary layout of 16 bits.
types.standardGuarantee:
Standard guarantee: if `uint16_t` exists, it has exactly 16 bits. Unsigned arithmetic is modulo 2^16.
The standard only requires it when the implementation has a suitable underlying type with that exact width and no padding bits.
specialRules: Existence is conditional on implementation support.
Overflow is defined wrap modulo 2^16.
Excellent for bit-level protocols and binary interchange.

types.availability: C: <stdint.h> if provided. C++: <cstdint> if provided.
types.definition: Exact-width signed integer typedef with exactly 32 value bits and no padding bits, if the implementation provides it.
types.summary: Preferred type when serialized layout, protocol fields, binary file formats, SIMD lane widths, or cross-language ABI contracts need an exact signed width of 32 bits.
types.standardGuarantee:
Standard guarantee: if `int32_t` exists, it has exactly 32 bits of width and two's-complement-style ordinary range behavior for that width on mainstream implementations.
The standard does not require every implementation to provide every exact-width typedef unless the implementation has a suitable underlying type.
specialRules: Existence is conditional on implementation support.
Best choice for wire formats and binary interchange when available.
Still participates in integer promotions.

types.availability: C: <stdint.h> if provided. C++: <cstdint> if provided.
types.definition: Exact-width unsigned integer typedef with exactly 32 bits, if the implementation provides it.
types.summary: Preferred unsigned exact-width type for packet headers, file formats, hashes, low-level bit manipulation, and stable binary layout of 32 bits.
types.standardGuarantee:
Standard guarantee: if `uint32_t` exists, it has exactly 32 bits. Unsigned arithmetic is modulo 2^32.
The standard only requires it when the implementation has a suitable underlying type with that exact width and no padding bits.
specialRules: Existence is conditional on implementation support.
Overflow is defined wrap modulo 2^32.
Excellent for bit-level protocols and binary interchange.

types.availability: C: <stdint.h> if provided. C++: <cstdint> if provided.
types.definition: Exact-width signed integer typedef with exactly 64 value bits and no padding bits, if the implementation provides it.
types.summary: Preferred type when serialized layout, protocol fields, binary file formats, SIMD lane widths, or cross-language ABI contracts need an exact signed width of 64 bits.

types.availability: C: <stdint.h> if provided. C++: <cstdint> if provided.
types.definition: Exact-width unsigned integer typedef with exactly 64 bits, if the implementation provides it.
types.summary: Preferred unsigned exact-width type for packet headers, file formats, hashes, low-level bit manipulation, and stable binary layout of 64 bits.
types.standardGuarantee:
Standard guarantee: if `uint64_t` exists, it has exactly 64 bits. Unsigned arithmetic is modulo 2^64.
The standard only requires it when the implementation has a suitable underlying type with that exact width and no padding bits.
specialRules: Existence is conditional on implementation support.

types.definition: Signed integer type with at least 8 value bits; chosen as the smallest supported type meeting that requirement.
types.summary: Portable fallback when exact width is not available or not required, but you need at least 8 bits.
types.standardGuarantee: Standard guarantee: exists if the implementation provides the <stdint.h>/<cstdint> family; width is at least 8 bits.
specialRules: The implementation may map this to a wider type than the exact-width family.

types.definition: Unsigned integer type with at least 8 value bits; chosen as the smallest supported type meeting that requirement.
types.summary: Portable fallback for bit counts / storage where at least 8 bits are required and exact width is not necessary.
types.standardGuarantee: Standard guarantee: width is at least 8 bits. Unsigned arithmetic is modulo 2^N for the actual width N.
specialRules: The implementation may map this to a wider type than the exact-width family.

types.definition: Signed integer type with at least 8 bits that the implementation considers efficient for ordinary use.
types.summary: Use when you care more about natural efficient arithmetic than about exact object size, while still needing at least 8 bits.
types.standardGuarantee: Standard guarantee: width is at least 8 bits; exact chosen type is implementation-defined.
specialRules: Often maps to the natural machine register width rather than the smallest width.

types.definition: Unsigned integer type with at least 8 bits that the implementation considers efficient for arithmetic.
types.summary: Useful for counters / masks where at least 8 bits are needed and native fast width is preferable.
types.standardGuarantee: Standard guarantee: width is at least 8 bits; exact chosen type is implementation-defined. Unsigned arithmetic wraps modulo 2^N of the actual width.
specialRules: May be much wider than the number in its name suggests.

types.definition: Signed integer type with at least 16 value bits; chosen as the smallest supported type meeting that requirement.
types.summary: Portable fallback when exact width is not available or not required, but you need at least 16 bits.
types.standardGuarantee: Standard guarantee: exists if the implementation provides the <stdint.h>/<cstdint> family; width is at least 16 bits.
specialRules: The implementation may map this to a wider type than the exact-width family.

types.definition: Unsigned integer type with at least 16 value bits; chosen as the smallest supported type meeting that requirement.
types.summary: Portable fallback for bit counts / storage where at least 16 bits are required and exact width is not necessary.
types.standardGuarantee: Standard guarantee: width is at least 16 bits. Unsigned arithmetic is modulo 2^N for the actual width N.
specialRules: The implementation may map this to a wider type than the exact-width family.

types.definition: Signed integer type with at least 16 bits that the implementation considers efficient for ordinary use.
types.summary: Use when you care more about natural efficient arithmetic than about exact object size, while still needing at least 16 bits.
types.standardGuarantee: Standard guarantee: width is at least 16 bits; exact chosen type is implementation-defined.
specialRules: Often maps to the natural machine register width rather than the smallest width.

types.definition: Unsigned integer type with at least 16 bits that the implementation considers efficient for arithmetic.
types.summary: Useful for counters / masks where at least 16 bits are needed and native fast width is preferable.
types.standardGuarantee: Standard guarantee: width is at least 16 bits; exact chosen type is implementation-defined. Unsigned arithmetic wraps modulo 2^N of the actual width.
specialRules: May be much wider than the number in its name suggests.

types.definition: Signed integer type with at least 32 value bits; chosen as the smallest supported type meeting that requirement.
types.summary: Portable fallback when exact width is not available or not required, but you need at least 32 bits.
types.standardGuarantee: Standard guarantee: exists if the implementation provides the <stdint.h>/<cstdint> family; width is at least 32 bits.
specialRules: The implementation may map this to a wider type than the exact-width family.

types.definition: Unsigned integer type with at least 32 value bits; chosen as the smallest supported type meeting that requirement.
types.summary: Portable fallback for bit counts / storage where at least 32 bits are required and exact width is not necessary.
types.standardGuarantee: Standard guarantee: width is at least 32 bits. Unsigned arithmetic is modulo 2^N for the actual width N.
specialRules: The implementation may map this to a wider type than the exact-width family.

types.definition: Signed integer type with at least 32 bits that the implementation considers efficient for ordinary use.
types.summary: Use when you care more about natural efficient arithmetic than about exact object size, while still needing at least 32 bits.
types.standardGuarantee: Standard guarantee: width is at least 32 bits; exact chosen type is implementation-defined.
specialRules: Often maps to the natural machine register width rather than the smallest width.

types.definition: Unsigned integer type with at least 32 bits that the implementation considers efficient for arithmetic.
types.summary: Useful for counters / masks where at least 32 bits are needed and native fast width is preferable.
types.standardGuarantee: Standard guarantee: width is at least 32 bits; exact chosen type is implementation-defined. Unsigned arithmetic wraps modulo 2^N of the actual width.
specialRules: May be much wider than the number in its name suggests.

types.definition: Signed integer type with at least 64 value bits; chosen as the smallest supported type meeting that requirement.
types.summary: Portable fallback when exact width is not available or not required, but you need at least 64 bits.
types.standardGuarantee: Standard guarantee: exists if the implementation provides the <stdint.h>/<cstdint> family; width is at least 64 bits.
specialRules: The implementation may map this to a wider type than the exact-width family.

types.definition: Unsigned integer type with at least 64 value bits; chosen as the smallest supported type meeting that requirement.
types.summary: Portable fallback for bit counts / storage where at least 64 bits are required and exact width is not necessary.
types.standardGuarantee: Standard guarantee: width is at least 64 bits. Unsigned arithmetic is modulo 2^N for the actual width N.
specialRules: The implementation may map this to a wider type than the exact-width family.

types.definition: Signed integer type with at least 64 bits that the implementation considers efficient for ordinary use.
types.summary: Use when you care more about natural efficient arithmetic than about exact object size, while still needing at least 64 bits.
types.standardGuarantee: Standard guarantee: width is at least 64 bits; exact chosen type is implementation-defined.
specialRules: Often maps to the natural machine register width rather than the smallest width.

types.definition: Unsigned integer type with at least 64 bits that the implementation considers efficient for arithmetic.
types.summary: Useful for counters / masks where at least 64 bits are needed and native fast width is preferable.
types.standardGuarantee: Standard guarantee: width is at least 64 bits; exact chosen type is implementation-defined. Unsigned arithmetic wraps modulo 2^N of the actual width.
specialRules: May be much wider than the number in its name suggests.

types.definition: Signed integer type capable of holding a converted object pointer round-trip when the implementation provides it.
types.summary: Useful for low-level runtime code, tagged pointers, FFI glue, and integer storage of object-pointer values when the implementation explicitly supports that contract.
types.standardGuarantee:
Standard guarantee: optional. If provided, it can represent a converted `void*` / object pointer value suitably for round-trip under the implementation's rules.
Width is usually pointer size.
specialRules: Availability is optional.
Pointer provenance / strict aliasing / round-trip semantics are subtler than raw width alone, especially in modern abstract machine discussions.
Do not assume function pointers convert the same way as object pointers.

types.definition: Unsigned integer type capable of holding a converted object pointer value when the implementation provides it.
types.summary: Common for bit manipulation on pointer values, address formatting, hashing support, and FFI bridges. Still a low-level type with sharp edges.
types.standardGuarantee: Standard guarantee: optional. If provided, it can represent a converted object pointer value. Exact width usually matches pointer width.
specialRules: Availability is optional.
A wide enough integer does not automatically preserve all semantic pointer properties in every optimization/provenance model.
Unsigned arithmetic wraps modulo 2^N.

types.definition: Widest signed integer type that the implementation provides in the standard typedef family.
types.summary: Useful for generic formatting, generic integer utilities, and code that wants 'largest standard signed integer type here' rather than an ABI-fixed width.
types.standardGuarantee: Standard guarantee: exists with the <stdint.h>/<cstdint> family. Exact width is implementation-defined and at least as wide as every other standard signed integer type provided in that family.
specialRules: May be the same as `long long`, `long`, or a wider extension-backed standard typedef depending on the implementation.
Not a substitute for arbitrary precision.

types.definition: Widest unsigned integer type in the standard typedef family.
types.summary: Good for generic unsigned integer utilities and generic formatting, but not a promise of 'largest possible integer in all extensions forever'.
types.standardGuarantee: Standard guarantee: exists with the family. Width is at least that of every other standard unsigned integer typedef in the family.
specialRules: Unsigned arithmetic wraps modulo 2^N.
May still be insufficient for some external big-number tasks.

types.definition: Standard single-precision floating type. On mainstream IEC 60559 / IEEE 754 implementations this is binary32.
types.summary: Typical choice when memory footprint or vector width matters more than maximum precision. About 6-7 reliable decimal digits on mainstream binary32 implementations.
types.standardGuarantee:
Standard guarantee: at least the minimum precision/range described by the language's floating characteristics macros / limits.
Exact IEC 60559 conformance is implementation-defined unless the implementation documents it; mainstream compilers on modern CPUs generally use IEEE 754 binary32 semantics.
types.precision:
On binary32 systems: 24 bits of precision including the implicit leading bit, approximately 6-7 decimal digits.
Exact integers are representable only up to 2^24 without gaps.
types.rounding:
Converting a mathematical real value or a higher-precision floating value to `float` generally rounds to the nearest representable value according to the active implementation/IEEE rounding mode.
On mainstream IEEE setups the default mode is round to nearest, ties to even.
specialRules: `0.1f` is not exactly representable in binary32.
Comparing floats for exact equality is often fragile unless exact representability is intended.
Overflow usually produces infinity on IEEE systems; exceptional behavior depends on FP environment / implementation model.
Subnormals, NaNs, signed zero, and infinities matter for edge cases.
commonConfusions: Decimal digits in source do not imply exact decimal storage.
A value may print as 0.1 yet not be exactly 1/10.

types.definition: Standard double-precision floating type. On mainstream implementations this is IEEE 754 binary64.
types.summary: The ordinary default floating type in most C and C++ code. Offers about 15-16 reliable decimal digits on mainstream binary64 systems and a much wider exact-integer range than `float`.
types.standardGuarantee: Standard guarantee: at least as much precision and range as `float`. Exact IEEE binary64 is not mandated by the core language everywhere, but it is the overwhelming mainstream reality.
types.precision:
On binary64 systems: 53 bits of precision including the implicit leading bit, approximately 15-16 decimal digits.
All integers with magnitude up to 2^53 are exactly representable.
types.rounding: Conversion to `double` rounds to representable values using the implementation's floating environment. On mainstream IEEE systems the default is round to nearest, ties to even.
specialRules: `0.1` is not exactly representable in binary64.
After 2^53, adjacent integers are no longer all representable.
NaN never compares equal to itself.
Signed zero can affect division and some branchless numeric code.

types.definition: Extended floating-point type with precision/range at least that of `double`. This is one of the most implementation-dependent arithmetic types in the language.
types.summary:
Meant to provide more precision or range than `double`, but the actual representation varies significantly by compiler, target, and ABI.
On x86 GCC/Clang it is often 80-bit extended precision stored in 12 or 16 bytes; on MSVC it is usually just an alias in behavior to `double`; on some platforms it may be IEEE binary128.
types.standardGuarantee:
Standard guarantee: at least as much precision as `double`. Exact format, size, padding, and encoding are implementation-defined.
Therefore portable code should not assume one universal width or one universal decimal-digit count.

mainstreamSizes.size: typically 16 bytes storage for 80-bit extended precision
8 bytes, usually same precision as double
may be 16 bytes / binary128 or double-double

types.precision:
Depends on implementation. Common cases:
- x87 extended precision: about 64 bits of significand precision.
- alias-to-double implementations: same 53-bit precision as `double`.
- binary128 implementations: 113 bits of precision.
types.rounding: Same broad rounding principles as other floating types, but intermediate precision, excess precision, and storage format behavior can vary with ABI, compiler options, and evaluation method.
specialRules: Never assume `sizeof(long double) > sizeof(double)` implies binary128.
Never assume `long double` has the same behavior across GCC/Clang/MSVC.
Serialization / ABI exchange of `long double` is notoriously non-portable.

types.availability: C floating extension family from TS/IEC 60559 integration; availability depends on compiler/library support. Not standard C++ core arithmetic types.
types.definition: _Float16 is part of the C floating extension family for explicitly named IEC 60559-oriented formats or extended formats.
types.summary: Use only when you intentionally target implementations that expose this family. Excellent for explicit FP-width intent, poor for broad portability unless you control the toolchain.
types.standardGuarantee: Availability and exact support depend on implementation. Where supported, the name corresponds to an intended IEC 60559 format family rather than the looser built-in `float`/`double`/`long double` portability model.
specialRules: Support varies significantly by compiler, target, library, and language mode.
Not generally a portable baseline for mixed C/C++ codebases.

types.availability: C floating extension family from TS/IEC 60559 integration; availability depends on compiler/library support. Not standard C++ core arithmetic types.
types.definition: _Float32 is part of the C floating extension family for explicitly named IEC 60559-oriented formats or extended formats.

specialRules: Support varies significantly by compiler, target, library, and language mode.
Not generally a portable baseline for mixed C/C++ codebases.

Not generally a portable baseline for mixed C/C++ codebases.

specialRules: Support varies significantly by compiler, target, library, and language mode.
Not generally a portable baseline for mixed C/C++ codebases.
